sentences.text: They wanted to get out.
They wanted to be-- see, they were behind barbed wire.
And I explained to them in German that we would slowly but surely release them, and they would enter a hospital, which we had established on, on, at Dachau.
And I explained to them why they-- we, they couldn't just open the gate and let them out.
Because they were so anxious to-- naturally, they would be, since they had been imprisoned probably for many years, most of them.
Oh, yes.
Absolutely.
It's hard to describe.
I recall it somewhat, but probably some were from the furnace, you know, which by that time, they, of course, were had been extinct.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was numb when I was there.
I'll be very honest with you.
I was not-- I was somewhat in a state of shock.
When I entered the camp, the moment we came in with the Jeep, and we-- we saw the freight cars at first.
They were outside on the, on the railway line.
And then I saw all these dead people laying there.
And I looked into the freight cars, and they were piled up on top of each other.
And there was a bucket in the middle, which they used for a latrine, and these dead people all around.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Hey, this is what-- 49, 50 years or something later, and it's still-- I still see it in front of my eyes.
It was a very shocking experience.
Very shocking.
I was just-- I was even hoping there would still be some, some guards that we could kill.
But by that time, by the time I got there, which was about 48 hours or thereabouts after liberation, those guards that we had not killed, they simply had run away.
But we killed a good number of them.
The chaplain was just as revolted by all this as I was, I'm sure.
He was-- we were both in a state of shock.
I'll be honest with you.
Really, we were.
We were there several hours.
I walked around, I saw everything.
And I, I walked off the beaten path, so to speak, where some of SS soldiers had been shot down.
I sort of kicked them with my boots, to turn them over.
But they were dead.
And I just wanted to be sure that they were dead.
And by afternoon or late afternoon, we left.
We went back our unit.
Yes, yes.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
Absolutely not.
I mean, all of this came out later on.
You know, the Germans hush-hushed their atrocities in the camps.
And I think most people probably thought they were just like internment camps.
That's what-- at least, that's the feeling I had, until we found out otherwise.
No, I did not.
If-- I doubt that they were performed at Dachau.
They might have been, but I didn't see any of it.
To our unit.
That's correct.
Oh, absolutely, oh, absolutely.
I told everyone within earshot what I saw.
Oh, sure.
Well, it depends on who you were telling this to.
I think the, the greatest impact was probably when I talked to my fellow Jewish soldiers.
There were a number in my unit.
But I think everyone was absolutely shocked by what they heard, Absolutely.
Oh, my goodness, yes.
We went-- I think, within about five days after Dachau was liberated-- maybe a week, not, it wasn't much longer-- the war had come to an end.
It might have been May 5, if I'm not mistaken.
May 8?
May 8.
We had, from that point on, gone further south, first of all, to Salzburg in Austria.
That's how far we went.
And then we, we were shipped-- our unit went back into Germany.
And the day the war ended, we were in a small, beautiful village, not too far from Switzerland, called Ettal, E-T-T-A-L.
My unit was stationed in that little town, the day the war ended.
Never.
That's the only camp I ever saw.
For quite a while yet.
And then our unit went to Stuttgart-- either Stuttgart or Goeppingen, one or the others, I'm not sure which came first.
And I think it was around September thereabouts, when we were told that we would be leaving for France.
And then we would be going home.
And that's exactly what happened.
There was sort of transit camp in France.
And then we went aboard a railroad car to Marseilles.
And we shipped home from Marseilles.
I certainly did.
That was on my mind all the time.
The moment we invaded Germany, I had but one thing on my mind-- to go back to Gelsenkirchen and to kill the head of the Gestapo of Gelsenkirchen.
That was with me from the day I entered Germany.
And I asked for permission, the moment Gelsenkirchen was liberated, to go back to Gelsenkirchen.
And my commanding officer said as follows-- I'm going to give you a three-day pass.
And I can't give it to you to go to Gelsenkirchen because it's in the British zone.
But you do what you want.
You know your way around, he said, just be back in three days.
And I did go back to Gelsenkirchen.
And I hit-- no, I think I took a car.
I took a Mercedes, of course.
I did.
I requisitioned a German car.
This was during the early summer of 1945.
And I drove to Gelsenkirchen.
And there was British occupiers-- British soldiers there.
No Americans were there.
I was the only American there.
And I had-- made a beeline for the, the, British occupation, British occupation office, where sergeant-- he looked to me like 6 foot 7, a giant of a man.
I asked him when I walked in-- I identified myself, told him that I used to live in this town.
I was Jewish.
And I wanted to know the whereabouts of the head of the Gestapo.
He goes over to a cabinet.
He picks up a book, a photo album.
And he opens it to page one.
And on page one, he had a picture of that bastard.
He said, you're looking for him?
I said, yes.
He said, so are we.
And it turns out that he was, naturally, the number one person wanted by the British.
And he was missing in action.
They told him that he was missing on the Russian front.
But I found out later on, almost all the Germans they were looking for, they said, they are missing on the Russian front.
Certainly, many of them did get killed on the Russian front.
But many of them did not.
They surfaced again.
They came--
So nevertheless, I went to the house that he lived in.
Because I spoke a perfect German.
And I knocked on doors.
And I asked the neighbors if they knew his whereabouts.
And I got the same answer everywhere-- that he is missing on the Russian front.
Then I located this fellow that was called the [NON-ENGLISH], who was the fellow who sat in my father's office.
Novak.
And he was practicing law again.
He was a lawyer, that was his profession in Germany at the time.
I had him arrested by the British.
They did take him in.
And in my presence, this big sergeant, he beat the living daylights out of him.
And he made him confess to having stolen thousands and thousands and thousands of dollars from my father's business.
And then he sent him to a concentration camp.
They had one set up for the Germans at the time, outside of a town called Recklinghausen.
They sent him there.
That was his punishment.
Yes, I did go back to Germany, so.
Well, to this day--
To this day, no, you don't.
You know, after a while, you kind of lose-- I never lost interest in seeing the man punished.
But I would think that he probably never surfaced.
I don't think he did, because eventually, he would have been wanted by the Germans, also.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
And his name, we-- it's hard to--
I'm trying desperately.
I could probably find out his name again.
If it's for the record, if you needed it, I could get it, OK?
At the moment, my mind is a blank as to what his name was.
I did.
The first home that we had lived in had been bombed to oblivion.
It was no longer standing.
And the second home, I walked into the apartment.
And other people-- Gentiles, of course, who didn't even know that Jews had ever lived in there-- were very polite.
And I just didn't have much to say to them, except I wanted to see the apartment.
At the time when we moved in, it was a brand new building.
But by that time, it looked sort of grimy to me, and-- I, I, yeah, I did see it.
But my primary purpose there also, then, was to go to the grave site of my mother.

That was my-- besides visiting, I mean, trying to capture this SOB, I visited my mother's grave site, I visited my brother's grave site, uncles, aunts, cousins, people who were in the Jewish cemeteries in either Essen or Gelsenkirchen.
I spent an entire day doing that the next day, before I went back to my unit.
They were in terrible shape.
They claimed-- and the tombstones had been turned over.
And they claimed, the German authority claimed a lot of this was due to bomb damage.
Some of it might have been.
But I would say that most of the headstones looked to me like they had been turned over, physically, by vandals, I would think, by-- my mother's gravestone had been turned upside down.
But I knew exactly where it was located, so I found it immediately.
And the grass was overgrown.
You know, nobody had taken care of the cemeteries.
It looked awful.
Not a soul, not-- I'm trying to think.
At that time, no.
When later on, many, many, many years later, we once went to France, my wife and I.
And then we also visited Germany for the second time.
I spoke to several people, including some Jews who had moved back.
And there was-- there were two survivors that I-- no, three survivors of the original German Jewish community in Gelsenkirchen.
All the other people I met-- there were a number Jews.
As a matter of fact, we had a service, a Friday evening service.
And we had barely a minyan.
But I would say that 7 out of 10 or 8 out of 10 were people who had been released from the camps, from Eastern Europe, and had made Germany their home.
Yeah, oh yeah.
I would have to look up.
But it was probably 20 years later.
Well, I was discharged from the army, shortly thereafter.
I was discharged in December of 1945.
Came home on the-- I think it was the 22nd of December, 1945.
I resumed my profession.
At first, I opened a dry cleaning store and tailor shop.
Five years later, I sold it.
And I formed a partnership with a fellow by the name of Erich Stern.
And he and I open a haberdasher, complete menswear store here, in the neighborhood of Hyde Park.
The store still exists.
It's now 44 years later.
Our oldest son more or less runs it, because at the age of 65, I semi-retired.
I'm pretty much fully retired now.
And--
The name is Cohn and Stern.
It's been in Hyde Park 44 years.
We also, 20 years ago, in addition to this store, opened a Hallmark card and gift shop, which our daughter manages and runs.
And I, I'm partners with both of our children in these two stores.
And then the children, on their own, both our son and daughter, opened a third store in Hyde Park.
It's called-- well, the Hallmark store is called Joyce's Hallmark, Joyce, the name of our daughter.
And the last store they opened was last year.
No, wait a minute, it was this year, I'm sorry, in May of 1945, called Fundamentals.
It's a store--
--In Hyde Park, in the middle of the center of the University of Chicago.
And it features primarily you know, casual clothing, Levis, things of that nature, similar to what a Gap store would carry.
Howard, the oldest son is Howard.
Our daughter is Joyce.
And our middle son is David, he's a physician.
He is the head of the Department of Infectious Diseases for the city of Denver.
He specializes in, in the field of AIDS and tuberculosis are those fields that he is primarily working in.
My wife's name is Elsa.
She's probably the most important person in my life, always has been, and always will be, hopefully.
Yeah, oh, yeah.
She's a terrific woman, absolutely.
She made me what I am today.
And I mean it, OK.
I mean it.
She's a great woman.
She worked in the business on and off.
Now, right now, being before the Christmas holiday, she's not here today.
She was here, but she's working today.
And I'm doing all this talking.
And she's doing all the work.
Well, she's helping.
We always do.
The moment I'm finished here, I'll leave for the store, and I'll give them a helping hand.
I hope they need it.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
Well, when I married, I married Elsa, who was from a more orthodox or conservative background than I came from.
I, I came from a liberal reform background.
And the moment I returned home, we joined the synagogue that her parents were members of.
My parents belonged to a more liberal reform synagogue.
But in deference to my wife's family and wishes-- and my own, I guess-- we joined a conservative synagogue.
The name of it was at that time Habonim.
And I became very active.
First I was on the board.
And then [BACKGROUND NOISE] for five years, I served as the president of Congregation Habonim.
Today it's Ezra-Habonim.
I'm still a member of Ezra-Habonim.
But I am also-- most of our activities, religious ones, are with Rodfei Zedek, the Hyde Park Congregation.
Oh, yes.
I, I, am doing-- because of my business affiliations in the neighborhood, I was president of the Chamber of Commerce for a number of years, and--
Yes, correct.
And I served on the boards of Savings and Loan Association.
And I have been a member of some of the local organizations.
But I've also been a member of American Jewish Congress, of B'nai B'rith, ORT, the JCC, and other Jewish organizations for many, many years.
And JUF, of course.
And HIAS.
We, we lived here after I came home from the service.
Then we moved to the South Shore area, where we had a lovely house for a number of years, or at least 10 years.
And then we decided to move back to Hyde Park.
We sold the house.
And we lived in this apartment, which is very comfortable, ever since.
About 27 years, I think, we've lived in this apartment now.
East End, 5000 East End, yes.
Well, I think Hitler's coming to power changed our life completely.

As I've mentioned earlier on in detail, the changes, I probably would have gone on through high school and more than likely, attended the university in Germany, which is what middle class Jewish parents-- like they do today, here in the states, just as well-- ask their children to do.
And I would have probably been some sort of an academic.
I think, I think my parents wanted me to be an attorney, I believe so.
So there's been a very dramatic turnaround.
And of course, I would have never met my wife, if all this would not have happened.
Even so she came from Germany, it was a really small town, Gross-Bieberau near Darmstadt, in the southern part of Germany.
I would have never met her.
And that would have been a great loss for me.
Because she is just an exceptional person.
Well, I would say they somewhat confirmed my belief in our background, in our religion.

Even so, I've had grave doubts-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] while I was growing up, and all during these last 60 years, I've had grave doubts that God is really-- that, that there really is a God because of what happened to our people.
Many times, I, I questioned why this all happened.
Can there be a God, Jewish or otherwise, that permits these sort of things to happen?
And I am very troubled by this.
I've stayed a Jew.
I, I couldn't be anything but.
Because even if I wouldn't be a Jew, I would still have the same questions-- why?
And I'm not a great believer.
I'll be honest with you.
I'm a Jew, but not a great believer.
But then, I have grandchildren today.
And one of my granddaughters, Amy, just wrote an essay, essay in school about my hero.
And she talks about God in a way that brought tears to my eyes, I'll be honest with you.
So it-- what comes around, goes around, in a way.
Yes, for five years.
Oh, yes.
I questioned it even while I was a president.
I think I was more of a-- of a president because I ran a good business.
I think the elders of the synagogue thought that the synagogue should be handled in a more businesslike manner.
And rather-- and I did not want to accept the presidency.
Because I was not all that religious, you see.
But they insisted that I would take it.
And when I left, they said I did a good job.
I hope I did.
I don't know.
I hope I did.
